topic: A person finds out their surprise party has been cancelled
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with the soft, unwelcome thud of a dropped stone. My stomach clenched, a tight, unpleasant knot forming just beneath my ribs. I clicked it open, the subject line—"Party Update"—a cruel joke. The words confirmed it: the surprise celebration for Sarah was off. I reread the text, the cheerful planning details now seeming like mocking confetti. A slow, cold wave washed over me, leaving a hollow ache in my chest. I stood up abruptly, the cheap carpet feeling suddenly abrasive beneath my bare feet. I walked to the window, staring out at the indifferent streetlights, feeling a profound sense of nausea rising with the air I breathed.

[story 2]

"Cancelled." The single word echoed in the silence of my apartment, sharp and brittle. I stared at the screen, my jaw tightening until my teeth ached. My hands, resting on the cool surface of the desk, began to tremble almost imperceptibly. It wasn't just disappointment; it was a churning, sour feeling that made the back of my throat constrict. I scrubbed a hand over my face, trying to wipe away the sudden flush creeping up my neck. Every detail of the event—the decorations, the coordinated outfits, the anticipation I’d carefully nurtured—now seemed tainted, cheapened by this abrupt retraction. I pushed back from the desk, the movement stiff and jerky, needing to escape the suffocating atmosphere of the room.

[story 3]

I read the notification three times, the text blurring slightly at the edges. A low, involuntary sound escaped my lips, something between a sigh and a suppressed snarl. The reality of the situation settled in, heavy and suffocating. My shoulders slumped, a complete surrender to the weight of the anticlimax. I felt a strange metallic taste bloom in my mouth. Looking around my living room, everything felt overly bright, the colors garish and insincere. I picked up my phone, not to reply, but just to feel the familiar coldness of the glass against my palm. The air in the room felt thick, stagnant, and utterly repellent.
